items.q: What was the Augustine vs Pelagius dispute about original sin?
items.correct: Augustine argued humans inherit a corrupted nature from Adam (original sin) and cannot achieve salvation without grace; Pelagius argued humans retain free will to choose good without divine intervention and that sin is imitated, not inherited.
items.misconception: Original sin means Christians believe babies are evil.
items.random_wrong: Pelagius and Augustine agreed humans are born innocent.
items.why: Original sin is about inherited nature/condition, not personal guilt of infants. The Pelagian controversy shaped Western Christianity — Augustine won, but the question of how far human nature is corrupted remains theologically contested.

items.q: What is the difference between Sunni and Shia Islam?
items.correct: They split over who should lead the Muslim community after Muhammad: Sunnis accepted Abu Bakr and elected caliphs; Shias believed leadership should follow Muhammad's bloodline through Ali.
items.misconception: Sunnis and Shias believe in different gods.
items.random_wrong: Sunnis follow the Quran and Shias follow the Bible.
items.why: Both share the Quran, prophet and core beliefs. The original split was political — about succession — and theological differences developed later. Students inflate it into a different religion.

items.q: What does karma mean in Hindu and Buddhist thought?
items.correct: The principle that actions have moral consequences shaping future experience or rebirth — not fate, but ethical cause and effect built into reality.
items.misconception: Karma is when bad things happen to bad people as punishment.
items.random_wrong: Karma is a god who decides what happens to you.
items.why: Students treat karma as cosmic justice meted out by a deity. In its philosophical origins it is impersonal moral causation — your own actions, not external judgement, shape your circumstances.

items.q: Which 2015 papal encyclical addresses care for the environment?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Laudato Si'. “Rerum Novarum” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which Jewish ethical principle prioritises saving life?
items.why: It's Pikuach nefesh. “Tzedakah” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In which year was Laudato Si' published?
items.why: The real figure is 2015. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
